playbooks.title: Repeated parent changes (parent churn)
playbooks.summary: A node keeps swapping parents in a short window. Usually a marginal RF link to its current parent, a parent that itself is unstable, or two routers with near-equal link cost causing flapping.
evidence_to_collect: query_timeline for the node over the last 2 hours with kinds=['parent_change']
Per-edge RSSI/LQI from get_neighbors of each candidate parent
Whether the candidate parents are themselves in any active issue
remediation_steps: Inspect physical placement: is the device on the edge of two routers' coverage?
Check that each candidate parent has healthy uplink (RSSI > -75, no high_error tag).
If both parents are healthy and far apart, prefer adding a mesh router in between rather than tuning.
If one parent shows weak_link/high_error to the device, reposition or replace it.

playbooks.title: Repeated attach failures
playbooks.summary: Node is attempting to attach to the mesh but cannot complete the handshake. Usually credentials drift, partition fragmentation, or RF.
evidence_to_collect: query_timeline kinds=['attach','attach_failed'] over last hour for the eui
Active partition state (get_partition_state) — multiple partitions is a red flag
Dataset rotation events in observer_events
remediation_steps: Confirm the device still has the active operational dataset (re-commission if a rotation happened).
Ensure at least one router is reachable from the device's physical location.
If multiple partitions exist, resolve the split first — attaches will fail across partitions.

playbooks.title: Node went offline
playbooks.summary: Node has not been seen for the offline threshold (default 30 min). May be powered off, out of range, on the wrong fabric, or — for SEDs — simply asleep with a parent timeout.
evidence_to_collect: query_timeline kinds=['status_change','parent_change','link_lost'] for the eui, last 24h
Last known parent and that parent's current health
Observer disruption windows overlapping the last_seen → now interval (already auto-applied as suppression)
remediation_steps: If the node is an SED: check its parent's child-timeout configuration vs. the device's poll period.
If the parent went down at roughly the same time, fix the parent first.
If this fired during an OTBR/Matter outage, the issue has already been downgraded — verify recovery on next tick.
Otherwise, physical inspection: power, distance, interference.

playbooks.title: Re-attach storm
playbooks.summary: Multiple routers witness the same neighbor re-attaching repeatedly. Strong signal that the device's MLE state is being reset (firmware bug, watchdog reboot, or a duplicate credential collision).
evidence_to_collect: query_events event_type='re_attached_node' for the eui
Whether other devices of the same manufacturer/model show the same pattern
Recent firmware update events in observer_events
remediation_steps: Check for a firmware update or rollback on the affected device.
Look for a duplicate operational identity (two devices with the same node id on different fabrics).
If a vendor firmware regression is suspected, downgrade one unit and compare.

playbooks.title: Mesh disagreement (OTBR vs router counters)
playbooks.summary: A router's self-reported MAC TX counter and the OTBR-witnessed counter differ by ≥25%. Indicates either a stale Matter cache, a frame ingress path the OTBR doesn't see (multi-BR), or a router with corrupted counters.
evidence_to_collect: Latest otbr_diagnostics row for the eui
Last 6 ticks of MAC counters for the router
Multi-BR state (are there two OTBR slugs?)
remediation_steps: If there are two OTBRs on the network, this is expected; consider tagging the router with the BR it belongs to.
If only one BR, restart the affected router as a first probe.
Persistent disagreement after restart suggests firmware counter corruption — file with vendor.

playbooks.title: Issue annotated as observer-suppressed
playbooks.summary: The issue fired during a window where we (the addon, OTBR, or Matter Server) were down or restarting. The reasoner downgraded crit → warn and attached suppressed_by evidence. Treat this as low-confidence until the next clean tick.
evidence_to_collect: evidence.suppressed_by[] on the open issue
query_timeline sources=['observer_events'] over the trigger window
remediation_steps: Wait one full pipeline tick after the suppressing window ends.
If the issue auto-closes, it was an artifact of the observer outage.
If it persists, treat it as a real issue per the rule's playbook.

playbooks.title: Thread partition split
playbooks.summary: Two partitions exist where there should be one. Routers in different partitions cannot forward to each other; SEDs anchored on the minority partition will appear offline to a controller bound to the majority partition.
evidence_to_collect: get_partition_state — both partition_ids and their member counts
list_topology_snapshots and diff_topology across the split moment
OTBR /node/router/{id} for the minority leader
remediation_steps: Confirm both partitions use the same active operational dataset.
Identify the physical RF gap between partitions and bridge it (add a router).
If one partition is stuck on a stale dataset, force-rejoin its leader.

playbooks.title: Real partition split (evidence-backed)
playbooks.summary: Multiple live partitions plus evidence of a device transitioning between partitions. This narrows diagnosis vs. a bare partition-count: it suggests an unstable boundary device or RF gap rather than stale IDs.
evidence_to_collect: Issue evidence.partitions and evidence.recent_partition_changes
get_mesh_state to confirm current partition membership
diff_topology across the split window (if snapshots exist)
remediation_steps: Identify the device(s) in evidence.recent_partition_changes; check their placement and power stability.
Bridge the RF gap between partitions (add/relocate a router) and re-run ingest.
If partitions disagree on extended_pan_id, resolve credentials drift (re-commission the minority leader).

playbooks.title: Dead-link reference (unknown neighbor EUI64)
playbooks.summary: A router references an EUI64 that is not present in the registry-backed nodes table, persisted across multiple ingestion ticks. This usually indicates a recommissioned device leaving stale neighbor/route cache entries.
evidence_to_collect: Issue evidence.references[] (reporter, unknown neighbor, source, seen_count)
get_neighbors for the reporter router to see link_established/allocated state
list_all_nodes to confirm the neighbor truly is absent
remediation_steps: If the neighbor is a known physical device, re-commission it so HA registry matches the mesh identity.
Restart the reporter router to flush stale neighbor cache entries.
If references persist and the neighbor is truly gone, inspect for duplicate identities or a stuck child table.

playbooks.title: Route to OTBR unreachable (loop/unknown next hop)
playbooks.summary: Walking the next-hop chain from a router to the OTBR terminates in a loop, unknown next hop, or missing route-table entry. This points at routing-table corruption, partition mismatch, or a missing upstream neighbor relationship.
evidence_to_collect: Issue evidence.route_walk (hops + issues)
get_mesh_state to confirm partitions and current links
get_neighbors for the router and its first upstream hop
remediation_steps: If the route walk reports different_partition, resolve the partition split first.
Restart the affected router to repopulate its route table.
If unknown_next_hop persists, inspect the router's neighbor table for missing upstream links and RF quality.

playbooks.title: Sleepy-end-device unexpected battery drain
playbooks.summary: SED is waking more often than its poll period would predict. Causes: parent child-timeout too short (forces frequent re-keep-alives), unicast traffic storms, or a controller subscription that exceeds the device's sleep duty cycle.
evidence_to_collect: Device's published poll period vs. parent's child-timeout
Number of Matter subscriptions targeting the device
MAC TX/RX counter rate-of-change per tick
remediation_steps: Increase the parent's child-timeout to at least 3× the device's poll period.
Consolidate or remove redundant subscriptions on the controller.
Verify the device firmware honors CSL or SSED if supported.

playbooks.title: Wi-Fi / Thread 2.4 GHz coexistence
playbooks.summary: Persistent weak-link/high-error tags across many edges suggest 2.4 GHz airtime is saturated. Thread channels 11–14 overlap Wi-Fi ch 1; 15–20 overlap Wi-Fi ch 6; 25–26 are usually safe.
evidence_to_collect: Current Thread channel (network_data)
Local Wi-Fi channel usage (out-of-band, if available)
Distribution of weak_link / high_error tags across links
remediation_steps: Move Thread to channel 25 or 26 if Wi-Fi is on ch 1/6/11.
Avoid co-locating the OTBR antenna within 1 m of a Wi-Fi router antenna.
Consider an external Thread radio with a directional or relocated antenna.

playbooks.title: Multiple border routers, inconsistent BR table
playbooks.summary: Multi-BR deployments are valid but require all BRs to publish consistent on-mesh prefixes, external routes, and BBR preference. Diverging tables cause SRP outages and unreachable services.
evidence_to_collect: network_data row per partition_id (BR servers, on-mesh prefixes)
Each BR's /node/network-data (REST)
SRP service-mDNS visibility from a wired client
remediation_steps: Make sure both BRs are on the same Thread Network Name and Extended PAN ID.
Verify BBR preference is sane (only one should be high).
If divergence persists, demote one BR and re-commission it from scratch.

playbooks.title: SRP service outage
playbooks.summary: SRP server is unresponsive or has stale registrations. Matter discovery and service routing degrade; nodes appear 'online' on the mesh but invisible to controllers.
evidence_to_collect: Latest network_data.services for each partition
OTBR log for 'srp_server' lines
Whether a recent BR restart correlates with the issue (observer_events)
remediation_steps: Restart the OTBR add-on; SRP server typically recovers within 60s.
If services don't re-register, force-clear and re-commission the offending devices.
Multi-BR networks must have exactly one SRP server elected — check election state.

playbooks.title: Active dataset channel mismatch
playbooks.summary: Two devices believe the network is on different channels (e.g. a partial dataset rotation succeeded). Symptoms: attach_failures spike, partition_id_change_count rises.
evidence_to_collect: Active dataset channel per partition (network_data.channel)
Per-node last-known channel from device_discovery diagnostics
Recent dataset rotation events
remediation_steps: Re-issue the dataset to the affected device via OTBR REST.
If many devices are affected, complete the in-flight rotation rather than reverting.

playbooks.title: Leader churn
playbooks.summary: Leader role is changing routers more than once per hour. Indicates an unstable mesh — usually a marginal leader candidate that drops out, forcing re-election.
evidence_to_collect: query_events event_type='leader_change' over last 6 hours
Stability counters of each leader-eligible router (detached_role_count, attach_attempt_count)
remediation_steps: Pin a stable router as preferred leader if your stack supports it.
Disable router-eligibility on flaky devices.

playbooks.title: Active dataset rotation in progress
playbooks.summary: An operator or commissioner is rolling the active dataset. Expect transient attach_failures and partition transitions for the duration; do not treat any single issue as actionable until rotation completes.
evidence_to_collect: Pending operational dataset present in network_data
active_timestamp deltas per partition
observer_events around the rotation start
remediation_steps: Wait for the rotation to complete (commissioner-defined timer).
Re-evaluate any open issues 10 minutes after the rotation timestamp passes.

playbooks.title: Phantom / stale-reference nodes
playbooks.summary: Routers are caching neighbor table entries to EUIs that no longer exist in the registry (decommissioned, re-credentialed, or never properly removed). Wastes cache slots and confuses topology analysis.
evidence_to_collect: list_phantom_nodes
links.neighbor_known=0 rows per reporter
remediation_steps: Use the HA deep-link path returned by list_phantom_nodes to remove the device entry.
Restart the reporter router to flush its cache.
If the EUI keeps reappearing, an unattached device with that EUI is still in RF range.

playbooks.title: Node on a different Thread network (stale credentials)
playbooks.summary: The node reports an extended_pan_id that differs from the rest of the mesh. The hardware is alive and the radio works, but it's joined a different (typically stale) Thread network. Common cause: device was factory-reset and re-commissioned while HA still had an older Thread dataset cached, so it picked up the wrong credentials.
evidence_to_collect: analyze_node for the affected eui — confirm node.extended_pan_id and node.network_name differ from peers
list_thread_datasets — does HA still have the stale dataset on file?
get_health_snapshot — summary.distinct_thread_networks > 1 confirms the split
remediation_steps: Open HA → Settings → Devices & Services → Thread, delete any non-preferred dataset whose extended_pan_id matches the minority node.
Factory-reset the affected device and re-commission via the Apple Home / Google Home / HA companion app so it adopts the preferred dataset.
After re-commissioning, the device's eui64 will change. Use analyze_node on the new eui to check for a duplicate physical_identity entry, and delete the stale registry row if so.
